info.title: NGINX One Console Spec
info.description: The API Reference documentation for NGINX One Console

namespace.description: The application namespace used to organize and scope API resources.

tenant_cname.description: The specific DNS name used to access your tenant.

tags.name: Data Plane Key
tags.description:
The `DataPlaneKey` object represents a secure token required to establish a connection between your NGINX instances and NGINX One. 
As a fundamental security measure, the data plane key ensures that only trusted NGINX instances can register and communicate with NGINX One. 

Data plane keys expire after one year. This is the default setting if you don't specify an expiration time when you create a key. 
If necessary, you can update the data plane key later to extend its expiration.

Revoking a data plane key will disconnect the associated NGINX instances from NGINX One.

**IMPORTANT** Data plane keys are not saved and are displayed only once when you generate them. You should save this key in a secure location for future reference.

tags.name: Instances
tags.description:
The `Instance` object represents an active NGINX installation. 
You can access detailed information about each NGINX instance, including its configuration analysis, security advisories, and operational status.

tags.name: Feature Flags
tags.description:
Get details about which feature flags are enabled in the NGINX One console. 
Feature flags are used to toggle the availability of application features or modules without changing code.

tags.name: Config Sync Groups
tags.description:
The `ConfigSyncGroups` object represents a NGINX config sync group where `Instances` are grouped to have same configuration. 
You can access detailed information about each NGINX config sync group, including its configuration analysis and operational status.

tags.name: Certificates
tags.description:
The `Certificates` object in the NGINX One console represents an SSL certificate, covering both managed and unmanaged types. 
You can view essential details like issuer, expiration status, and the instances or config sync groups where each certificate is deployed.

tags.name: CVEs
tags.description: The `CVEs` object represents the security advisories published for all the F5 products supported by NGINX One.

tags.name: Events
tags.description: Get a list of system events in the NGINX One console.

tags.name: Staged Configs
tags.description: The `StagedConfigs` object represents a NGINX staged config which users can save to publish at a later time.

tags.name: Control Planes
tags.description:
The `Control Planes` object represents an external control plane such as NGINX Ingress Controller or NGINX Gateway Fabric.
From this endpoint, you can get detailed information about each control plane, including its NGINX instances, configurations, security advisories, and operational status.

tags.name: Metrics
tags.description: Get system metrics for your NGINX data plane instances. These metrics are collected by the NGINX Agent and reported to NGINX One.

tags.name: Settings
tags.description:
Configuration option for different aspect of NGINX One service.
You can set NGINX Instance cleanup preferences.

tags.name: Usage
tags.description: Usage information

tags.name: Chatbot
tags.description: This API allows you to interact with the chatbot assistant

tags.name: Inventory
tags.description: Get tenant usage information for NGINX Plus data plane instances. These metrics are collected by the NGINX data plane instances and reported to NGINX One Console.

tags.name: NGINX App Protect
tags.description: Manage and publish security policies on your NGINX data plane instances.

tags.name: WAF Log Profiles
tags.description: Manage WAF log profiles for security logging and monitoring.

tags.name: Deployments
tags.description: Manage NAAS deployments.

tags.name: Load Test
tags.description: Load Test actions

tags.name: Templates
tags.description:
* Manage NGINX configuration templates — import, create, copy, list, retrieve, update, delete templates and their versions.
* Generate a standalone NGINX configuration snippet for an individual augment template.
* Compose a base template with augment templates to preview or render a full NGINX configuration, optionally saving it as a staged config.
* Manage template submissions — create, list, retrieve, update (full or per-template), and delete submissions that record applied template inputs for NGINX configuration generation.

To publish the configuration, use the `PUT /instances/{instanceObjectID}/config` endpoint.

patch.summary: Generate an analysis report for the provided modified configuration
patch.description:
Analyzes the provided partial updates to an existing NGINX configuration and generates a report detailing potential issues along with optimization suggestions. 
This analysis accounts for additive updates made to `NginxConfig`. To delete files, omit the `file.contents` field. 
This method compares the provided `config_version` with the current NGINX instance configuration to detect conflicts, which may arise if the `config_version` does not match due to an out-of-band update. 
Note that this operation is for analysis purposes only and does not apply any changes to the configuration. 
The report is not stored and is provided only in the API response.
To publish the configuration, use the `PATCH /instances/{instanceObjectID}/config` endpoint.

200.description: Successfully analyzed the provided NGINX configuration.

400.description: Request cannot be processed due to invalid input or parameters. Verify the request format and provided data.

404.description: The NGINX instance with the specified object_id was not found. Check that the object_id provided is correct and corresponds to an existing resource.

put.summary: Publish a configuration to an instance
put.description:
Publishes a new or updated NGINX configuration to the specified instance.
In the specified `configs`, empty files are not allowed in the directory.
If no existing configuration is found, a new one is created; otherwise, the current configuration is overwritten. 
Before publishing, use the `PUT /instances/{instanceObjectID}/config-report` endpoint to generate an analysis report for the provided configuration.
You can specify `payloads` in the request to deploy managed certificates and keys to the dataplane. Include file paths
for each payload component. 
Additional details:
  * `conf_path` is optional. When provided, it must be an absolute path that references a file present in the provided configurations. When omitted, the system uses the conf_path from the existing instance metadata.

202.description: The request to publish the configuration has been accepted and is being processed. To check the publication status, make a GET request to /instances/{instanceObjectID}/publications.

400.description: Request cannot be processed due to invalid input or parameters. Verify the request format and provided data.

404.description: The NGINX instance with the specified object_id was not found. Check that the object_id provided is correct and corresponds to an existing resource.

500.description: An unexpected error occurred on the server. Please try the request again later.

patch.summary: Apply partial updates to an instance's configuration
patch.description:
Applies the specified partial updates to an existing NGINX configuration. 
This endpoint accepts additive updates to `NginxConfig`. 
To delete files, omit the `file.contents` field. 
This method compares the provided config_version with the current NGINX instance configuration to detect conflicts, which may arise if the config_version does not match due to an out-of-band update. 
Before publishing, use the `PATCH /instances/{instanceObjectID}/config-report` endpoint to generate an analysis report for the modified configuration.
Additional details:
  * `conf_path` is optional. When provided, it must be an absolute path that references a file present in the provided configurations. When omitted, the system uses the conf_path from the existing instance metadata.

202.description: The request to publish the configuration has been accepted and is being processed. To check the publication status, make a GET request to /instances/{instanceObjectID}/publications.

400.description: Request cannot be processed due to invalid input or parameters. Verify the request format and provided data.

post.summary: Parse and validate an SSL certificate
post.description:
Parses and validates an SSL certificate. 
It checks the provided PEM files and verifies that the public certificates follow the correct X.509 format. 
If the certificate cannot be parsed, an error will be returned. 
Otherwise, as long as the certificate is parsable, a `200 OK` status will be returned even if there are issues 
such as mismatched private keys or expired certificates. Details of any issues found will be shown in the "warnings" field of the response.

200.description: Successfully parsed and validated the SSL certificate.

400.description: Request cannot be processed due to invalid input or parameters. Verify the request format and provided data.

500.description: An unexpected error occurred on the server. Please try the request again later.

get.description:
Retrieves the details for an SSL certificate, including:
* Object ID that uniquely identifies this certificate object
* SSL certificate type (managed or unmanaged by NGINX One Console)
* Certificate type (whether it is a CA bundle or a certificate-key pair)
* Subject name of the leaf certificate, or the soonest-expiring CA in a bundle
  * This subject name will be the DNS name in the SAN extension of the certificate. If not present, it will be the certificate's common name
* Status of the certificate (valid, expiring, expired)
* Validity period, if applicable to multiple certificates
* Metadata for each public certificate if multiples are provided
* Private key metadata, if available

200.description: Successfully retrieved the details of the SSL certificate.

404.description: The SSL certificate with the specified object_id was not found. Check that the object_id provided is correct and corresponds to an existing resource.

500.description: An unexpected error occurred on the server. Please try the request again later.

delete.description:
* Deletes a managed SSL certificate from the NGINX One console. This operation is disabled for unmanaged certificates, as they get cleaned up automatically when they are not used in any NGINX configuration. 
* An optional flag `deleteFromDataPlanes` when set to true, can be used to remove the certificate from data plane instances to where it was deployed.
  * Deleting from data planes triggers publications on either instances or Config Sync Groups. Creates a new version for a template or updates an existing draft version, based on the provided content and the state of the latest version:

- If the latest version is a **draft** and the content has changed, the draft is updated in place.
- If the latest version is **final** and the content has changed, a new draft version is created.
- If the content is unchanged (matches the latest version), no changes are made and the latest version is returned.

The response indicates whether a new version was created/updated (`201 Created`) or if the content was unchanged (`200 OK`).

post.summary: Submit templates for previewing NGINX configuration.
post.description:
Submits a set of templates for rendering NGINX configuration.

The `preview_only` query parameter controls how the request is processed:
  - When `preview_only` is `true`, the API validates template parameters and renders the full NGINX configuration for preview **without creating a template submission object**. (**Currently, only this mode is supported.**)
  - When `preview_only` is `false` or omitted, the API is intended to render and create a submission. 
    - If no target object ID is provided, a new staged config will be created.

200.description:
Returned only when the `preview_only` flag is set to `true`.
Responds with the rendered NGINX configuration that can be used for preview or to create/replace a staged configuration manually.

202.description:
The template submission has been accepted and is being processed.
Publication status(es) can be retrieved by querying the appropriate publications endpoint for the target object.

put.description:
Performs a full update (replacement) of an existing template submission by
replacing all input values and payloads, then re-rendering the NGINX configuration.

The `preview_only` query parameter controls how the request is processed:
  - When `preview_only` is `false` (default), the API re-renders, persists the updated values,
    and re-publishes to the submission's existing targets.
  - When `preview_only` is `true`, the API re-renders the NGINX configuration
    with the new values for preview **without persisting** the changes.

200.description:
Returned only when `preview_only` is `true`.
Responds with the re-rendered NGINX configuration using the updated input values.

202.description:
Returned when `preview_only` is `false`.
The updated submission has been accepted and re-published to existing targets.

409.description: The submission cannot be completed due to one or more template validation errors. See the error details for more information.

Partially updates an existing template submission by replacing the input values
for a single template identified by `templateObjectID`. All other templates in
the submission retain their existing values, and the full NGINX configuration is
re-rendered using the merged values.

Payloads and target objects are preserved from the existing submission and cannot
be changed through this endpoint.

The `preview_only` query parameter controls how the request is processed:
  - When `preview_only` is `false` (default), the API re-renders, persists the updated values,
    and re-publishes to the submission's existing targets.
  - When `preview_only` is `true`, the API re-renders the NGINX configuration
    with the new values for preview **without persisting** the changes.

200.description:
Returned only when `preview_only` is `true`.
Responds with the re-rendered NGINX configuration using the updated input values.

202.description:
Returned when `preview_only` is `false`.
The updated submission has been accepted and re-published to existing targets.

409.description: The submission cannot be completed due to one or more template validation errors. See the error details for more information.

post.description:
Returns attack analytics with optional dimensional breakdown.

**Response behavior:**
- Without `group_by`: Returns `total` count for the time range
- With `group_by`: Returns breakdown for that dimension, `total` is omitted

**Response structure by dimension type:**
- **Fixed enum dimensions** (`request_status`, `request_method`): Returns object with known keys and counts
- **Dynamic dimensions** (`ip`, `country`, `policy`, `url`, etc.): Returns array of items with cross-dimension context in `distinct` object

**Filter behavior:**
- All filters are combined with AND logic
- Use the `in` operator to achieve OR within a single dimension

by_request_outcome_reason.summary: Get breakdown by request outcome reason

filtered_top_ips.summary: Top 10 attacking IPs for blocked requests

200.description: Successfully returned attack analytics.

post.description:
Returns attack analytics as time-series data for charting.

**Response behavior:**
- Without `group_by`: Returns `total` series (aggregate counts over time)
- With `group_by`: Returns dimensional breakdown series, `total` is omitted

Data is returned with each dimension value as a separate series array,
making it easy to plot directly. The bucket size is determined by the
`resolution` field or auto-selected based on the time range.

**Supported dimensions for time-series:**
- `request_status` - Returns `blocked`, `alerted`, `passed` series

**Time alignment:** All series arrays contain the same timestamps, aligned to the
resolution boundary. Buckets with no events are zero-filled.

**Filter behavior:**
- All filters are combined with AND logic
- Use the `in` operator to achieve OR within a single dimension

200.description: Successfully returned attack analytics time series.

without_group_by.summary: Without group_by - returns total series only

with_group_by.description:
All series have aligned timestamps. Note the zero-filled bucket at 02:00
for `passed` where no events occurred.

no_data.summary: No data in time range - returns zero-filled buckets
no_data.description:
When no attacks occurred in the queried time range, time buckets are still
generated based on the resolution with count as 0.

no_data_with_group_by.summary: No data with group_by - returns zero-filled series for each dimension
no_data_with_group_by.description:
All dimension values have aligned timestamps with count: 0.
This ensures charts can render empty series correctly.

get.description:
Returns a paginated list of security events.

**Filtering:**
- Use the `filter_fields`, `filter_values`, and `filter_ops` parameters to filter events by various dimensions
- All filter parameters must have matching array lengths
- `support_id` is unique per event, so filtering by it returns a single-item list
- Time range filtering via `start_time` and `end_time` is recommended to limit results
- Multiple filter values can be chained using the `|` character (e.g., `blocked|alerted`)

**Pagination:**
- Results are paginated using standard Nginx One Console pagination
- Results are sorted by timestamp descending (most recent first) by default

**Response:**
- Returns summary fields suitable for list display
- Use `GET /app-protect/events/{id}` for full event details including raw request

parameters.name: start_time
parameters.description:
Beginning of the time range (inclusive).
Accepts ISO 8601 format or relative offset (e.g., `now-24h`).

parameters.name: end_time
parameters.description:
End of the time range (exclusive).
Defaults to current time if not specified.

200.description: Successfully returned list of security events.

get.description:
Returns a specific security event by its support ID.

**Response:**
- Returns full event details including:
  - Request metadata (headers, method, URL, response code)
  - WAF enforcement details (policy, outcome, violation_rating)
  - All triggered violations with context
  - All matched signatures with details
  - Raw HTTP request (if available and not truncated)
  - Threat campaign associations

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The support ID of the security event.

200.description: Successfully returned the security event.

TemplateSubmissionPreviewOnly.name: preview_only
TemplateSubmissionPreviewOnly.description:
Optional flag to control how a template submission request is processed.
  - When `true`, the request renders the full NGINX configuration for preview **without creating a template submission object** (this is currently the only supported mode).
  - When `false` or omitted (the default), the request is intended to render the configuration **and create a submission object**, but this feature is **not supported yet** and will return an error.
Note: Currently, only preview (stateless render) mode is supported. Submission creation is not yet implemented.

Set an expiration date and time for the data plane key in ISO 8601 format: YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ. 
If an expiration date isn't specified, the key will expire one year after it's created. 

You can use the _Update a data plane key_ endpoint to extend the expiration date.
It's not possible to update the expiration date once the data plane key has expired.

CertificateStatus.description:

  * `expiring` -  The certificate will expire within the next 30 days. Consider renewing it to maintain uninterrupted service.
  * `expired` - The certificate is no longer valid. Immediate renewal is recommended to ensure secure connections.
  * `not_ready` - The certificate is not ready to be used, based on the start date of its validity period.

aux.description:
An array of auxiliary directory contents related to the NGINX configuration. When auxiliary contents are
provided, they become the authoritative source of non-NGINX configuration content. Please ensure the
provided contents are complete, missing files that are referenced in the NGINX configuration can cause

NginxConfigPayload.description:
Details of Aux File Payload that goes with an NGINX configuration. Provide hints for the backend system on 
additional file contents that should be part of the NGINX Config Publication.
Can be used to deploy files related to SSL certificates, WAF policies and WAF log profiles to a data plane instance.

type.description:
Types of Aux File Payload:
  - inline_secret     - indicates the provided content for the payload should be stored in a secret location, and removed after the publication is done.
  - inline_content    - indicates the provided content for the payload should be stored, and removed after the publication is done. Note, the contents may end up in the `aux` content if used in this NGINX configuration.
  - unmanaged_certificate - indicates certificate content for an unmanaged certificate detected from a data plane instance through NGINX configurations. Will be filtered and ignored in the payload deployment.
  - managed_certificate - indicates public certificates managed by NGINX One Console.
  - managed_key - indicates a private key managed by NGINX One Console.
  - nap_policy_version - indicates a version of WAF policy managed by NGINX One Console.
  - nap_log_profile - indicates a WAF log profile managed by NGINX One Console.

NginxConfigPayloads.description:
An array of payloads that define which objects should be deployed and the file paths where each object will be placed on the data plane instance.
* When the type is `managed_certificate`, `managed_key`, `nap_policy_version`, or `nap_log_profile`, you must specify an `object_id`. 
The `object_id` must refer to a managed object.
* The NGINX One Console manages deployed file paths only for managed certificates and keys. If you don't want 
them to be managed by NGINX One Console, `inline_content` and `inline_secret` can be used for certificates or 
keys respectively, with `contents`. When you retrieve certificate deployment details, 
only the file paths of managed certificates and keys will be shown.
* If you use `inline_content` and `inline_secret` in your NGINX configuration, the NGINX One Console 
will detect them. When they are used as SSL directives of the NGINX configuration 
for certificates and keys, the certificates will be listed as `unmanaged_certificate` in the certificate 
deployment details.

* `payload` - The publication was successful, but there were warnings reported by attached payloads, see message for more details.

public_certs.description:
Base64-encoded PEM-formatted certificate information. 
This is used for updating an existing certificate object. The schema is the same as `CertificateContent`,
the only difference is that both `public_certs` and `private_key` fields are optional. There are three use
cases for this schema:
* the below update can be done on either a Cert Key Pair or a CA Bundle:
  * when only `public_certs` is populated, update the public certificates on a certificate object. 
  The updated public certificates will be validated against the existing private key.
* the below update can be done only on a Cert Key Pair:
  * when only `private_key` is populated, update only the private key on a certificate object. 
  The updated private key will be validated against the existing public certificates.
  * when both `public_certs` and `private_key` fields are populated, update both of them on a certificate 
  object.

private_key.description:
Base64-encoded private key string for the leaf certificate, required only for certificate-key pairs to 
verify the certificate's authenticity.

CertificateUpdateRequest.description:
Request structure for updating a certificate object. If key provided, it will be validated against the 
existing leaf certificate stored under the certificate object.
* Update for an unmanaged certificate object:
  * This converts the unmanaged certificate object to managed.
  * `public_certs` should always be provided during the conversion.
  * When key is provided, this certificate object is converted to a managed Cert Key Pair. Otherwise, it is
  converted to a managed CA Bundle.

CertificateDeployment.description:
Response structure containing certificate deployment details for an SSL certificate, which include
  * `association_type` represents type of the object affected by this certificate deployment, which is either
  an instance or config sync group
  * `object_id` represents the object ID for the associated instance or config sync group
  * `name` for either the host name of an instance or the name of a config sync group
  * `deployment_status`:
    * `latest`: deployment is up to date with the latest updated certificate and key contents
    * `stale`: deployment for either certificates or key is outdated, requires a redeployment with the latest contents
  * `cert_paths` represents the file paths used for deploying public certificates of this certificate object
  * `key_paths` represents the file paths used for deploying the private key of this certificate object, if a
  private key is present

StartTime.description:
Sets the beginning of the time period for your metrics query (inclusive).

Usage:
* `start_time` is required if `end_time` is specified.
* If `start_time` isn't provided, the API returns the latest metrics.
* `start_time` is required for aggregated metrics in order to calculate the `resolution` (granularity).

Time can be specified in two ways:
* Using ISO 8601 format. For example, "2019-08-07T09:57:36.088757764Z".
* As an offset from the current time. For the offset, use `+` or `-`, followed by a number and the appropriate time unit. The time unit can can be `y` (years), `M` (months), `w` (weeks), `d` (days), `h` (hours), `m` (minutes), or `s` (seconds). 
Example of an offset: "now-3h" (3 hours before now).

EndTime.description:
Sets the end point for the time period for your metrics query (non-inclusive).

Usage:
* Must be greater than `start_time`.
* If `start_time` is specified and `end_time` is not, `end_time` defaults to the current time.

Time can be specified in two ways:
* Using ISO 8601 format. For example, "2019-08-07T09:57:36.088757764Z".
* As an offset from the current time. For the offset, use `+` or `-`, followed by a number and the appropriate time unit. The time unit can can be `y` (years), `M` (months), `w` (weeks), `d` (days), `h` (hours), `m` (minutes), or `s` (seconds). 
Example of an offset: "now-3h" (3 hours before now).

MetricAggregation.description:
Static list of aggregation functions that can be applied to a compatible metric.
  * min
  * max
  * sum
  * avg
  * rate
  * accum_rate

MetricDimensions.description:
List the dimensions to include in the response for each metric series.

Usage:

* Specify the list of dimensions. Dimensions not specified in this parameter will be hidden in the results.
* If you specify dimensions in `group_by`, you don't need to list them again in `dimensions`. 
However, if you are using `group_by`, then any dimensions you list in `dimensions` must also be included in `group_by`.
  * To return a single series, specify the metric name with aggregation (for example, `{ "name": "agent.cpu.system", "aggregate": "avg" }`) and leave the `dimensions` parameter empty.

GroupByDimensions.description:
Group the query results by the specified dimension(s).

Usage:
* Specify the list of dimensions.
* For `group_by` to work, all metrics in the `names` parameter must be aggregated.

TopXMetricDimensions.description: List additional dimensions to include in the response for each metric series. The dimension specified by `group_series_by` will be included by default.

MetricDimension.description:
Static list of all metric dimensions:
  * `display_name` - Display name of the NGINX instance.
  * `file_path` - Path to the file.
  * `parent_hostname` - Hostname of the NGINX Plus instance.
  * `instance_object_id` - Unique ID of the instance registered with NGINX One Console.
  * `location_zone` - Name of an HTTP location zone.
  * `mount_point` - Filesystem mount point.
  * `namespace` - Namespace for the metric data.
  * `network_interface` - Server network interface.
  * `nginx_id` - Unique ID of the NGINX instance running on the data plane.
  * `server_zone` - Name of an HTTP or Stream server zone.
  * `system_id` - Unique ID of the operating system running nginx-agent.
  * `tenant` - Tenant for the metric data.
  * `csg_object_id` - Unique ID of the Config Sync Group registered with NGINX One Console.
  * `mode` - Variant value for metric `system.cpu.utilization`.
  * `state` - Variant value for metrics `system.filesystem.usage`, `system.memory.usage`.
  * `io_direction` - Variant value for metric `system.network.io`.
  * `status_range` - Variant value for metric `nginx.http.response.count`.
  * `logical_number` - Variant value for metrics that return a processor number.
  * `outcome` - Variant value for metrics that return an outcome.
  * `upstream_zone` - upstream zone for the metric data.
  * `upstream_name` - upstream name for the metric data.
  * `peer_state` - Variant value for metric peer state for the metric `nginx.http.upstream.peer.count`.
  * `peer_health_check` - Variant value for metric peer health check for the metric `nginx.http.upstream.peer.health_checks`.
  * `peer_address` - peer address for metric data.
  * `peer_name` - peer name for metric data.
  * `ecp_object_id` - Unique ID of the External Control Plane registered with NGINX One Console.

resolution.description:
Specifies the level of granularity for time series data in your results. Applicable only for endpoints that return time series data.

Usage: 
* Specify as a string with a number followed by a unit of time, such as `y` (years), `M` (months), `w` (weeks), `d` (days), `h` (hours), `m` (minutes) or `s` (seconds).
* Metrics in `names` must be aggregated.
* `start_time` is required.
* If `resolution` is not set, the API returns the maximum resolution (`end_time` - `start_time`).

order_by.description:
List the order by for dimension(s).

Usage:

* Must be a dimension included by `dimensions` or `group_by`.

series_limit.description:
Sets the maximum number of series that can be returned.                

Notes:
* Always returns an additional series with a dimension named `all`, aggregating the values of all metrics included in the results.
* A series with a dimension named `other` may be returned, aggregating the values of metrics not included in the results.

MetricNames.description:
Specify the metrics you want details for.

Usage: 
* List multiple metrics as json objects.
  * You can aggregate metrics with `avg`, `sum`, `min`, `max`, `rate`.
* Metrics with aggregates require a `start_time`.
* If you combine aggregated and non-aggregated metrics in a single query, any `group_by` clause applies only to the aggregated metrics.

MetricFilters.description:
Filter results based on dimension operations against one or more values.

Usage:
* Format as one or more predicates by providing all required elements.
  * `dimension`: The dimension name you want to filter on.
  * `operator`: The possible operators (`=`, `!=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `in`, `not`) you can use for comparison or condition checking.
  * `value`: Case sensitive value of the dimension to filter against.

For more complex filtering:

* Specify a `predicate` for logical expressions (`AND`,`OR`). 
* Use a wildcard `*` in the `value` for matching partial values.

MetricFilterSet.description: Encapsulates one or more `MetricFilter` object(s) to be grouped together.

operator.description:
Static list of all operations supported by filtering

* The `=`, `!=` only use the first element of the `values` array. Wildcards for partial matching is supported.
* The `in` and `not` both use all elements in the `values` array. Wildcards for partial matching is NOT supported.

values.description: Single value used for all operators except `in` and `not`.

OrderBy.description:
Sort order of the metric series in your results.

Usage:
* Provide all required elements.              
  * `direction`: The sorting direction either `desc` or `asc`.
  * `dimension`: The dimension for ordering.

OrderSeriesBy.description:
Sort order of the metric series in your results.

Usage:
* Provide all required elements.              
  * `direction`: The sorting direction either `desc` or `asc`.
  * `aggregate`: The aggregating function.

MetricStartTime.description:
The start time of your metrics query.

Usage:
* `start_time` is required if `end_time` is specified.
* If `start_time` and `end_time` isn't provided, the API returns metrics from the current time to the month before the current time.
* The `start_time` cannot be older than 120 days before the current time.

You can set the `start_time` in these ways:
* In ISO 8601 format. For example, "2019-08-07T09:57:36.088757764Z".
* As an offset from the current time. For the offset, use `+` or `-`, followed by a number and unit [`y` (years), `M` (months), `w` (weeks), `d` (days), `h` (hours), `m` (minutes), or `s` (seconds)]. 
* Example of an offset: "now-3h" (3 hours before now).

MetricEndTime.description:
The end time of your metrics query.

Usage:
* Must be greater than `start_time`.
* The time difference between `start_time` and `end_time` should be greater than an hour.
* The default `end_time` is the current time.
* The `end_time` cannot be older than 120 days before the current time.

You can set the `end_time` in these ways:
* In ISO 8601 format. For example, "2019-08-07T09:57:36.088757764Z".
* As an offset from the current time. For the offset, use `+` or `-`, followed by a number and unit [`y` (years), `M` (months), `w` (weeks), `d` (days), `h` (hours), `m` (minutes), or `s` (seconds)]. 
* Example of an offset: "now-3h" (3 hours before now).

- `augment`: An augment template that extends or modifies an existing NGINX configuration defined by a base template.

TemplateContextPath.description:
Specifies the full hierarchical context path within the NGINX configuration, using '/' as a separator.
* "main": Refers to the top-level context of the NGINX configuration.
* "http": Indicates the `http` block within the main context.
* "http/server": Indicates the `server` block within the `http` context.
* "http/server/location": Indicates the `location` block within the `server` context of the `http` block.
* "http/upstream": Indicates the `upstream` block within the `http` context.
* "stream": Indicates the `stream` block within the main context.
* "stream/server": Indicates the `server` block within the `stream` context.
* "stream/upstream": Indicates the `upstream` block within the `stream` context.

TemplateImportRequest.description:
A request to import a template into the system. This can be either a *base template* or an *augment template*,
as determined by the `type` field in the request body.

Base templates define the structure of an NGINX configuration and may include hook points for augment templates
using the `augment_includes` custom Go template function. This function accepts two parameters:
1. **context** (required, string): The target NGINX context path where augment content will be inserted (e.g. `"http"`, `"http/server"`, `"stream/server"`).
2. **label** (optional, string): A target label (e.g. `"primary"`) used for deterministic placement of an augment template's content within the specified context during NGINX configuration generation.

Augment templates are reusable configuration snippets that can be applied to specific NGINX contexts
within a base template, such as `http`, `http/server`, `stream` or `stream/server`. These can also make use of
hook points to other augment templates.

Nginx One supports custom Go template functions for advanced configuration generation.
See [Template Functions](https://yourdocs.com/templates/functions) for a complete list of supported functions.

file.description:
A .tar.gz archive containing a template and any necessary supporting files for generating an NGINX configuration.

The archive must include the following at its root:
- A template file (e.g., reverse_proxy_base.tmpl) written in valid Go template syntax for NGINX.
- If the template uses input variables, a schema file is also required. This file defines and validates the expected input data.
- The schema file could be a JSON schema or YAML (e.g., schema.json, schema.yaml).

File structure:
├── reverse_proxy_base.tmpl          # Required Go template file
├── schema.json or schema.yaml       # Required only if input variables are used

TemplateCreationRequest.description:
A request to create a template in the system. This can be either a *base template* or an *augment template*,
as determined by the `type` field in the request body.

Base templates define the structure of an NGINX configuration and may include hook points for augment templates
using the `augment_includes` custom Go template function. This function accepts two parameters:
1. **context** (required, string): The target NGINX context path where augment content will be inserted (e.g. `"http"`, `"http/server"`, `"stream/server"`).
2. **label** (optional, string): A target label (e.g. `"primary"`) used for deterministic placement of an augment template's content within the specified context during NGINX configuration generation.

Augment templates are reusable configuration snippets that can be applied to specific NGINX contexts
within a base template, such as `http`, `http/server`, `stream` or `stream/server`. These can also make use of
hook points to other augment templates.

Nginx One supports custom Go template functions for advanced configuration generation.
See [Template Functions](https://yourdocs.com/templates/functions) for a complete list of supported functions.

TemplateSnippetRenderRequest.description:
Defines a request to render a snippet of NGINX configuration for a specific template as standalone, without the need for a base template.

### Validations and Constraints:
- The template must specify its `target_context`.
- Input values for each template are passed independently via the `values` 
  object, and validated against each template's Schema.

### Processing:
- The system validates that the template is compatible with the specified target context.
- The final composed configuration is validated to ensure correctness and prevent conflicts or misconfigurations.

### External Template Documentation:
For more information on template functions and best practices, refer to:
  - [Template Function Reference](https://yourdocs.com/templates/functions)
  - [Template Authoring Guide](https://yourdocs.com/templates/guide)

TemplateSubmissionRequest.description:
Defines a request to render an NGINX configuration by combining a single base template 
with zero or more augment templates.

### Validations and Constraints:
- All templates referenced by Object ID must be available in the system before submission.
- Only one base template can be submitted.
- The base template must explicitly use custom Go function `augment_includes ("<context_path>", .)` to apply `augments`.
- Its not required to include all augments in the request that base template supports.
- The order of augments in the list determines the order in which they are rendered and applied.
- Each augment must specify its `target_context`, indicating where it should be applied in 
  the base template.
- Input values for each template are passed independently via the `values` 
  object, and validated against each template’s Schema.
- The `payloads` for auxiliary files only accepts types of `managed_certificate` and `managed_key`.

### Processing:
- The `base_template` defines the starting point of the NGINX configuration rendering.
- `conf_path` determines where the rendered configuration from base and augments should be placed within the NGINX directory structure.
- Each `augment` template is applied in the order provided, inserted at the appropriate target context
  using the `augment_includes ("<context_path>", .)` function declared in the base template.
- The system validates that each augment is compatible with the specified target context.
- The final composed configuration is validated to ensure correctness and prevent conflicts or misconfigurations.

### External Template Documentation:
For more information on template functions and best practices, refer to:
  - [Template Function Reference](https://yourdocs.com/templates/functions)
  - [Template Authoring Guide](https://yourdocs.com/templates/guide)

AugmentTemplateSubmissionRequest.description:
Defines a request to apply an augment template to a specific target context within the base template.

This extends the `BaseTemplateSubmissionRequest`. Additionally adding,
- `target_context`: Specifies the context within the base template where this augment should be applied.
- `child_augments`: Optionally, an ordered list of nested augment templates to apply as children of this template.

target_label.description:
Optional label that identifies a specific section in the base template hierarchy (e.g., "primary").

If provided, the value must match the label parameter used by the base template’s `augment_includes` functions; otherwise, the augment will not be applied.

Base response wrapper for analytics queries.

allOf.description: Request body for querying attack analytics.

Dimension to aggregate results by.

**Fixed enum dimensions** (returned as objects with known keys):
* `request_status` - blocked, alerted, passed
* `request_method` - GET, HEAD, POST, PUT, DELETE, CONNECT, OPTIONS, TRACE, PATCH

**Simple array dimensions** (returned as arrays with code/count):
* `response_code` - HTTP response codes

**Dynamic dimensions** (returned as arrays with cross-dimension context):
* `ip`, `country`, `policy`, `url`, `hostname`

**Response behavior:**
- If omitted: Returns `total` count only
- If specified: Returns dimensional breakdown, `total` is omitted

filter.description:
Pre-aggregation filter conditions.
All filters are combined with AND logic.
Use the `in` operator to achieve `OR` within a single dimension.

allOf.description: Request body for querying attack analytics time series.

Dimension to break down time-series by.
Limited to fixed enum dimensions for performance.

* `request_status` - Returns `blocked`, `alerted`, `passed` series
* `request_outcome_reason` - Returns the underlying reason for the outcome.

**Common `request_outcome_reason` values:**
- `SECURITY_WAF_OK`: Legitimate request, no violations.
- `SECURITY_WAF_VIOLATION`: Request blocked due to signature or protocol violation.
- `SECURITY_WAF_FLAGGED`: Violation detected, but request allowed (Transparent/Alert-only mode).
- `SECURITY_WAF_VIOLATION_TRANSPARENT`: Request would have been blocked, but the policy/signature is in staging/transparent mode.
- `SECURITY_NGINX_VIOLATION`: Request rejected due to NGINX-level constraints (e.g., malformed headers).
- `SECURITY_WAF_BYPASS`: Request was explicitly allowed due to an allow-list or bypass rule.

**Response behavior:**
- If omitted: Returns `total` series only
- If specified: Returns dimensional breakdown, `total` is omitted

RequestStatusCountsResponse.description:
Attack counts grouped by WAF enforcement status.
Each property represents a distinct status with its count.

blocked.description: Count of attacks that were blocked by the WAF.

alerted.description: Count of attacks that were alerted but not blocked (transparent mode).

passed.description: Count of attacks that passed through with no violations.

RequestOutcomeReasonCountsResponse.description: Total counts grouped by the underlying request outcome reason for the enforcement decision.

SECURITY_WAF_OK.description: Total requests passed with no security violations.

SECURITY_WAF_VIOLATION.description: Total requests blocked due to a policy or signature violation.

SECURITY_WAF_FLAGGED.description: Total violations detected but allowed (Policy in Transparent/Alert mode).

SECURITY_WAF_VIOLATION_TRANSPARENT.description: Total violations detected but allowed (Individual signature in Staging).

SECURITY_NGINX_VIOLATION.description: Total requests rejected due to NGINX-level parsing errors.

SECURITY_WAF_BYPASS.description: Total requests explicitly allowed via allow-lists or bypass rules.

RequestMethodCountsResponse.description:
Attack counts grouped by HTTP request method.
Each property represents an HTTP method with its count.

get.description: Count of attacks using GET method.

post.description: Count of attacks using POST method.

put.description: Count of attacks using PUT method.

delete.description: Count of attacks using DELETE method.

patch.description: Count of attacks using PATCH method.

connect.description: Count of attacks using CONNECT method.

head.description: Count of attacks using HEAD method.

options.description: Count of attacks using OPTIONS method.

trace.description: Count of attacks using TRACE method.

AccuracyCountsResponse.description:
Signature hit counts grouped by accuracy level.
Each property represents an accuracy level with its count.

low.description: Count of signature hits with low accuracy.

medium.description: Count of signature hits with medium accuracy.

high.description: Count of signature hits with high accuracy.

RiskCountsResponse.description:
Signature hit counts grouped by risk level.
Each property represents a risk level with its count.

low.description: Count of signature hits with low risk.

medium.description: Count of signature hits with medium risk.

high.description: Count of signature hits with high risk.

critical.description: Count of signature hits with critical risk.

ResponseCodeItem.description: Attack counts for a specific HTTP response code.

count.description: Number of attacks resulting in this response code.

DistinctCounts.description:
Cross-dimension counts providing context about the attacks for a given dimension value.
Each field represents a COUNT(DISTINCT ...) aggregation.

For example, when grouping by IP address:
- `ips` will be 1 (the IP itself)
- `urls` shows how many different URLs were targeted from this IP
- `violations` shows how many different violation types were triggered
- `policies` shows how many different WAF policies were matched

ips.description: Count of distinct source IP addresses associated with this dimension value.

violations.description: Count of distinct violation types detected.

policies.description: Count of distinct WAF policies that were triggered.

AttacksQueryResponse.description:
Response for attack analytics query.

**Response behavior:**
- Without `group_by`: Returns `total` count and `threat_campaigns` (distinct count)
- With `group_by`: Returns dimensional breakdown only (e.g., `request_status`), `total` and `threat_campaigns` are omitted

The `total`/`threat_campaigns` and dimensional breakdowns are mutually exclusive.

total.description: Total attack count for the query. Only present when no `group_by` is specified.

threat_campaigns.description:
Count of distinct threat campaigns in the queried time range.
Only present when no `group_by` is specified.

ip.description: Attack counts grouped by client IP address. Sorted by count descending.

country.description: Attack counts grouped by source country. Sorted by count descending.

policy.description: Attack counts grouped by WAF policy name. Sorted by count descending.

url.description: Attack counts grouped by request URL. Sorted by count descending.

response_code.description: Attack counts grouped by HTTP response code. Sorted by count descending.

hostname.description: Attack counts grouped by NGINX instance hostname. Sorted by count descending.

threat_campaign.description: Attack counts grouped by threat campaign name. Sorted by count descending.

TimeSeriesDataPoint.description: A single data point in a time series for a given dimension.

timestamp.description: The start time of this bucket.

count.description:
The count for this time bucket.
Represents the pivot entity type:
- **For attacks endpoint**: Count of attack events
- **For signatures endpoint**: Count of signature hits (pattern occurrences)

RequestStatusTimeSeries.description:
Time-series data grouped by WAF enforcement status.
Each property contains an array of data points for that status.

blocked.description: Time series of blocked attack counts.

alerted.description: Time series of alerted attack counts (transparent mode).

passed.description: Time series of passed attack counts (no violations).

RequestOutcomeReasonTimeSeries.description:
Time-series data grouped by the specific outcome reason for the enforcement decision.
Each property contains an array of data points representing that specific reason's count over time.

SECURITY_WAF_OK.description: Request passed with no security violations detected.

SECURITY_WAF_VIOLATION.description: Request blocked due to a confirmed signature or protocol violation.

SECURITY_WAF_FLAGGED.description: Violation detected, but allowed due to policy-level transparent mode.

SECURITY_WAF_VIOLATION_TRANSPARENT.description: Individual signature or violation was in staging/transparent mode and did not block.

SECURITY_NGINX_VIOLATION.description: Request rejected due to NGINX-level constraints (e.g., malformed headers).

SECURITY_WAF_BYPASS.description: Request was explicitly allowed due to an allow-list or bypass rule.

AttacksTimeSeriesQueryResponse.description:
Response for attack analytics time-series query.

**Response behavior:**
- Without `group_by`: Returns `total` series only (array of data points)
- With `group_by`: Returns dimensional breakdown only (e.g., `request_status`), `total` is omitted

The `total` and dimensional breakdowns are mutually exclusive.

**Time alignment:** All series arrays contain the same timestamps, aligned to the
resolution boundary. Buckets with no events are zero-filled to ensure consistent
data points across all series for chart rendering.

total.description: Total attack counts over time. Only present when no `group_by` is specified.

* `risk` - low, medium, high, critical

**Dynamic dimensions** (returned as arrays sorted by count descending):
* `signature` - Individual signatures with id, name, accuracy, risk
* `cve` - CVE identifiers

**Response behavior:**
- If omitted: Returns `total` (signature hits) and `unique` (distinct signatures)
- If specified: Returns dimensional breakdown, `total` and `unique` are omitted

ViolationsMetricField.description:
Count metric for violation context group_by queries.

* hits: Total occurrence count (default)
* unique: Distinct violation types per context

ViolationsDimensionField.description:
Available dimensions for grouping and filtering violation analytics.

* violation: Primary violation type (e.g., "Attack signature detected", "Illegal meta character in value")
* sub_violation: Sub-category or additional violation classification providing more granular detail. A value of "N/A" indicates no additional information.
* context: Location where violation was detected (header, cookie, parameter, uri, request, other)
* context_key: Field name within the detection context (cookie name, header name, parameter name)
* context_value: Field value that triggered the violation

allOf.description: Request body for querying violation analytics.

Dimension to aggregate results by.

**Fixed enum dimensions** (returned as objects with known keys):
* `context` - cookie, header, parameter, request, uri, other

**Dynamic dimensions** (returned as arrays with cross-dimension context):
* `violation` - WAF policy violation types (e.g., "Attack signature detected")
* `sub_violation` - Specific subtypes providing granular detail (e.g., "SQL-Injection" or "Missing Mandatory Host Header"). Returns "N/A" if not applicable.

**Response behavior:**
- If omitted: Returns `total` (violation occurrences) and `unique` (distinct violation types)
- If specified: Returns dimensional breakdown, `total` and `unique` are omitted

Count metric when `group_by=context`. Omit for default (`hits`).

* `hits`: Total violation occurrence count per context
* `unique`: Distinct violation types per context

filter.description:
Pre-aggregation filter conditions.
All filters are combined with AND logic.
Use the `in` operator to achieve `OR` within a single dimension.

Supported filter dimensions include attack-level dimensions (policy, ip, url, etc.)
in addition to violation-specific dimensions.

ViolationDistinctCounts.description:
Cross-dimension counts providing context about violations for a given dimension value.
Each field represents a distinct count aggregation.

ips.description: Count of distinct source IP addresses associated with this violation type.

urls.description: Count of distinct URLs where this violation was triggered.

policies.description: Count of distinct WAF policies that triggered this violation.

ViolationDimensionItem.description: A single item in a violation dimensional breakdown.

value.description:
The violation or sub-violation name.
Examples: "Attack signature detected", "Illegal meta character in value"

count.description: Total number of times this violation occurred within the queried time range.

ViolationContextCountsResponse.description:
Violation counts grouped by context type.
Indicates where in the HTTP request the violation was detected.

cookie.description: Count of violations detected in cookie values.

header.description: Count of violations detected in HTTP headers.

parameter.description: Count of violations detected in request parameters (query string or body).

request.description: Count of violations detected in the request body or general request context.

uri.description: Count of violations detected in the URI/URL path.

ViolationsQueryResponse.description:
Response for violation analytics query.

**Response behavior:**
- Without `group_by`: Returns `total` (total violation occurrences) and `unique` (distinct violation types)
- With `group_by`: Returns dimensional breakdown only (e.g., `violation`, `context`), `total` and `unique` are omitted

The aggregate fields (`total`, `unique`) and dimensional breakdowns are mutually exclusive.

total.description:
Total violation occurrence count for the query.
Only present when no `group_by` is specified.

unique.description:
Count of distinct violation types triggered.
Only present when no `group_by` is specified.

violation.description:
Violation counts grouped by violation type.
Sorted by count descending.

sub_violation.description:
Violation counts grouped by sub-violation type.
Sorted by count descending.

context_key.description:
Violation counts grouped by detection context and field name.
Sorted by count descending. Only present when `group_by=context_key`.

context_value.description:
Violation counts grouped by detection context and field value.
Sorted by count descending. Only present when `group_by=context_value`.

ViolationContextKeyItem.description: A single entry from a context_key dimension query.

context.description: Detection context (cookie, header, parameter, uri, request, other).

context_key.description: Field name within the context (e.g. cookie name, header name, parameter name).

context_value.description: Most representative field value for this key.

count.description: Number of times this key was involved in a violation.

ViolationContextValueItem.description: A single entry from a context_value dimension query.

context.description: Detection context (cookie, header, parameter, uri, request, other).

context_value.description: Field value that triggered the violation.

count.description: Number of times this value was involved in a violation.

BillingUsageEventsResponse.description: Paginated response for billing usage events query.

items.description: List of billing usage events matching the query.

BillingUsageEvent.description:
A single billing usage event containing NGINX information, usage metrics, NGINX metrics,
NGINX deployment details, JWT token information, and event metadata.

nginx_uid.description: Unique identifier for the NGINX instance.

deployment_cluster_node_count.description: Number of nodes in the deployment cluster.

sub.description: JWT subject claim identifying the subscription associated with this usage event.

f5_sat.description: F5 service activation time expiry time, as a Unix epoch timestamp.

aud.description: JWT audience claim identifying the intended recipient of the token.

iss.description: JWT issuer claim identifying the entity that issued the token.

iat.description: JWT issued-at time as a Unix epoch timestamp.

dedup_version.description: Deduplication version timestamp assigned for storage-level upsert/deduplication.

SourceType.description:
Source type of the billing usage event.
* `SOURCE_TYPE_NGINX_ONE` - NGINX One
* `SOURCE_TYPE_NIM` - NGINX Instance Manager

NAPStatus.description:
NGINX App Protect status of the instance.
* `NAP_STATUS_INACTIVE_OR_UNSPECIFIED` - Not specified or inactive
* `NAP_STATUS_ACTIVE` - Active

ProductType.description:
Deployment product type.
* `PRODUCT_TYPE_UNSPECIFIED` - Not specified is treated as being NGINX+
* `PRODUCT_TYPE_NIC` - NGINX Ingress Controller
* `PRODUCT_TYPE_NGF` - NGINX Gateway Fabric

messages.content: What is a dog and a cat?

choices.text:
A dog is a domesticated mammal, a member of the species Canis lupus familiaris. Dogs are often referred to as "man's best friend" due to their long history of companionship with humans. They are highly social animals and thrive on interaction with their human families and other dogs. Dogs come in a wide range of sizes, shapes, and breeds, each with its unique characteristics and needs.

A cat is a domesticated mammal, a member of the Felidae family. Cats are often independent and self-sufficient animals, but they also enjoy companionship with their human families and other cats. They are agile and graceful animals, known for their ability to jump high and climb trees. Cats come in a wide range of sizes, shapes, and breeds, each with its unique characteristics and needs.

Both dogs and cats have been domesticated for thousands of years and have become beloved companions for many people around the world. They each have their unique personalities and needs, and choosing between a dog and a cat ultimately comes down to personal preference and lifestyle.
